\title{Test Chapter: Data Structures Overview}
\begin{document}
\maketitle

\section{Introduction to Arrays}
An array is a contiguous block of memory that stores elements of the same type.
We can access any element in $O(1)$ time using its index.

\subsection{Array Indexing}
Most languages use zero-based indexing: the first element is at index 0.

\section{Introduction to Linked Lists}
A linked list stores elements in nodes connected by pointers.
Traversal is $O(n)$ because we must follow pointers from head to tail.

\begin{equation}
T(n) = O(n)
\end{equation}

\begin{verbatim}
struct Node {
    int data;
    Node* next;
};
\end{verbatim}

\section{Stacks and Queues}
Both stacks and queues are abstract data types built on top of arrays or linked lists.

\end{document}
